surface.name: Hello World Java sandbox charge
surface.selection_basis: Authorize.net labels Hello world as its step-by-step guide to make a first API call and defines that call as charging a test credit card and printing an authorization code and transaction ID. Java is the first language tab, so this record follows the Java sample plus the linked sandbox signup and credential instructions.

alternatives_considered.name: API Reference live console
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is positioned as a no-setup API explorer and does not measure the account, credentials, SDK, and local-code path.

alternatives_considered.name: C# Hello World
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is the second language tab and uses .NET tooling rather than the first-listed Java path.

alternatives_considered.name: Python, Ruby, and PHP Hello World
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: They are peer language implementations with different dependencies, sample repositories, and commands.

alternatives_considered.name: Accept Hosted
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It adds form-token, browser payment form, customer input, redirect or iframe, and receipt behavior.

entry_point.developer_intent: Create an Authorize.net sandbox account and run the Java Hello World program that submits a test card charge.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has no Authorize.net sandbox account or merchant API credentials.
The developer chooses Java because it is the first language implementation shown.
A Java development environment, Maven, Git, and a terminal are available.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Make the first API call by charging a test credit card.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: The Java sample sends an authCaptureTransaction to the Authorize.net sandbox and receives a successful transaction response.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The console prints Successfully created transaction with Transaction ID, response and message codes, description, and Auth Code.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Hello World page says Time to make your first API call and describes its payment Hello World as charging a test card and printing the auth code and transaction ID.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: The successful transaction ID and authorization code are the guide's promised first-call output. Testing error conditions, settlement, refunds, payment forms, and production merchant setup are separate downstream work.

prerequisites.requirement: An Authorize.net sandbox account; a merchant account is only required to accept real payments.
The sandbox API Login ID and Transaction Key for merchant authentication.
Java, Maven, Git, and a terminal for the first-listed sample path.
Access to the sandbox Merchant Interface, GitHub or Maven dependencies, and the Authorize.net sandbox API.

primary_path.action: Open Authorize.net Hello world.
details: The page directs the developer to create a sandbox account and offers Java, C#, Python, Ruby, and PHP implementations.
primary_path.input: Intent to make a first Authorize.net API call.
primary_path.output: Sandbox entry and language-specific first-call instructions.
primary_path.success_signal: Create sandbox account and Java Step 1 are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Select Create sandbox account.
details: The sandbox simulates card-network connections and does not process real payments.
primary_path.input: Intent to create a test account.
primary_path.success_signal: The required account fields are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Enter the required personal and work context.
details: Enter first name and last name.
Select Country/Region.
Enter email.
Select Developer type: Student, Open-source, Freelance, Commercial, or Corporate.
Select Job level: Individual Contributor, Manager, Director, or Executive.
primary_path.success_signal: No required field message remains for these values.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing values are rejected as required.

primary_path.action: Enter the required sandbox organization and currency values.
details: Enter Sandbox company name.
Select one of the listed Sandbox currencies.
The common setup guide says currency cannot be changed later; a different currency requires a new sandbox account.
primary_path.input: Sandbox company label and intended test currency.
primary_path.success_signal: Company and currency validation messages clear.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Choosing the wrong currency requires another sandbox account later.

primary_path.action: Create the sandbox login credentials.
details: Choose a login ID of 8 to 20 characters with letters and numbers and no symbols.
Choose a password of 12 to 20 characters containing lowercase, uppercase, a number, and a special character.
Enter the same password in Confirm password.
primary_path.input: Unique login ID and compliant password.
primary_path.success_signal: Login and password validation messages clear.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Invalid format, duplicate login, or mismatched passwords prevent submission.

primary_path.action: Accept the Authorize.net Sandbox Account Agreement.
details: Agreement acceptance is required.
primary_path.success_signal: The terms validation message clears.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Declining the agreement blocks account creation.

primary_path.action: Complete reCAPTCHA.
details: The form marks reCAPTCHA as required.
primary_path.success_signal: The CAPTCHA requirement clears.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A failed or expired challenge must be retried.

primary_path.action: Click Create Account.
primary_path.input: All required fields, agreement, and reCAPTCHA.
primary_path.success_signal: Account creation succeeds and sandbox access becomes available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The page does not document post-submit confirmation, email verification, or account-creation errors.

primary_path.action: Sign in to the sandbox Merchant Interface.
details: Use https://sandbox.authorize.net/ and the login created during signup.
primary_path.success_signal: Account navigation is available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Any account activation or email-verification step is not described on the signup or Hello World pages.

primary_path.action: Navigate to Account, Settings, then API Credentials & Keys.
details: The API Login ID and Transaction Key identify the account for gateway calls and are not Merchant Interface login credentials.
primary_path.success_signal: API Login ID and transaction-key controls are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Click New Transaction Key.
primary_path.success_signal: The key request can be submitted.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Click Submit to request a new transaction key.
primary_path.success_signal: The interface offers a PIN request.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Request and verify the PIN.
details: PIN verification is required before the Transaction Key is displayed.
primary_path.input: Access to the account's verification channel.
primary_path.success_signal: The Merchant Interface reveals the new Transaction Key.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The guide does not identify the delivery channel, wait time, retry controls, or expiration behavior.

primary_path.action: Copy and securely store the API Login ID and Transaction Key.
details: The API Login ID is 8 to 25 alphanumeric characters.
The Transaction Key is 16 alphanumeric characters.
Both are merchant authentication secrets for API access.
primary_path.success_signal: Both values are available for the Java sample.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Losing or mishandling the key requires generating another one.

primary_path.action: Select the Java Hello World tab.
details: Java is the first language option on the page.
primary_path.success_signal: Get the Authorize.net Java sample code is visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Selecting another language changes SDK, code, and run commands.

primary_path.action: Get the Authorize.net Java sample-code project from the linked repository.
details: For an existing project, the guide instead says to add net.authorize:anet-java-sdk:LATEST to the POM.
primary_path.input: Git and network access, or an existing Maven project.
primary_path.output: Local Java sample project with Authorize.net SDK dependency.
primary_path.success_signal: The sample source and pom.xml are available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Clone, Maven, or repository access errors must be resolved.

primary_path.action: Configure the Java sample to use the sandbox API Login ID and Transaction Key.
details: The sample's run method accepts apiLoginId and transactionKey and uses them in MerchantAuthenticationType.

The current page visibly includes example credential literals; use the credentials belonging to the created sandbox account because the credential guide defines those as account authentication values.
primary_path.input: Own sandbox API Login ID and Transaction Key.
primary_path.output: Java sample configured for the developer's sandbox account.
primary_path.success_signal: MerchantAuthenticationType receives the developer's credentials.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The Hello World prose does not explicitly state the substitution action, and using wrong credentials produces an API error.

primary_path.action: Confirm the sample targets Environment.SANDBOX.
details: The sample explicitly sets the Authorize.net environment to sandbox.
primary_path.success_signal: ApiOperationBase uses Environment.SANDBOX.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A production environment with sandbox credentials fails and could risk a real transaction if live credentials were used.

primary_path.action: Confirm the documented test payment and transaction request.
details: Use test card 4242424242424242 and expiration 0835.
Use amount 25.00 from main.
Set transaction type to AUTH_CAPTURE_TRANSACTION.
The optional customer email is test@test.test.
Assemble MerchantAuthenticationType, PaymentType, TransactionRequestType, and CreateTransactionRequest.
primary_path.input: Sandbox credentials and documented test data.
primary_path.success_signal: The source contains merchant authentication, test card, amount, payment, transaction type, and request assembly.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Malformed or unsupported request data returns a transaction or API error.

primary_path.action: Have the sample create the controller, execute the request, and obtain the API response.
details: Create CreateTransactionController with the request.
Call execute, then getApiResponse.
The code branches among success, failed transaction, API error, and null response.
primary_path.success_signal: A non-null response has resultCode OK and transaction messages.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The sample prints Failed Transaction, error code and message, or Failed to get response for the documented failure branches.

primary_path.action: Build the Java sample with Maven.
details: Run mvn package.
primary_path.success_signal: Maven completes and creates target/SampleCode.jar.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Compilation, dependency, or test failures stop the build.

primary_path.action: Run the ChargeCreditCard sample.
details: Run java -jar target/SampleCode.jar ChargeCreditCard.
primary_path.input: Packaged JAR, network access, and configured credentials.
primary_path.success_signal: The program receives the sandbox response and prints result details.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Credential, network, validation, or payment errors print the sample's failure output.

primary_path.action: Verify that the test charge succeeded.
details: Confirm Successfully created transaction with Transaction ID is printed.
Confirm Response Code, Message Code, Description, and Auth Code are present.
primary_path.success_signal: A transaction ID and authorization code are printed on the success branch.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Use the printed error code and error message with Authorize.net's response-code reference if the success fields are absent.

branches.condition: The developer chooses C#, Python, Ruby, or PHP.
branches.path: Follow the corresponding SDK or sample-repository setup, insert sandbox credentials, use its documented test-card transaction code, run its language-specific command, and verify its success output.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The auth-capture outcome remains a transaction ID and authorization response, while prerequisites, code, and commands change.

branches.condition: The developer adds Authorize.net to an existing Java project.
branches.path: Add net.authorize:anet-java-sdk:LATEST to pom.xml and copy or adapt the ChargeCreditCard code instead of cloning the full sample project.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Avoids the sample repository but requires project integration and source-file setup.

branches.condition: The developer uses the API Reference live console.
branches.path: Open Charge a Credit Card, select a request format, edit the out-of-box sandbox sample, send it, and inspect the response.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Avoids local account and SDK setup, so it measures the API explorer rather than owned credentials and code.

branches.condition: The developer needs to accept a real payment.
branches.path: Obtain a merchant account, generate production API credentials, change the environment and endpoint, complete go-live and PCI work, and use real payment data.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds underwriting, merchant, compliance, credential, and production-safety gates.

friction_gates.description: Signup requires developer type, job level, sandbox company, country, and a currency that cannot later be changed.
The sandbox login ID and password have explicit length and complexity constraints.
The Sandbox Account Agreement must be accepted.
A requested PIN must be received and verified before the Transaction Key is shown.
API Login ID and Transaction Key are required and must be stored securely.
The Hello World requires a language selection; this record uses first-listed Java.
Java, Maven, Git or equivalent source retrieval, and SDK dependencies are required.
Sandbox environment, account credentials, test-card data, and request construction must align.

sources.title: Authorize.net Hello world
sections_used: Step 1: Get the Authorize.net Java sample code
evidence_supported: Complete Java SDK, request, response, build, and run path

sources.title: Common setup questions
sections_used: What is an API login ID and transaction key?
How do I obtain the transaction key for my sandbox account?
What features are not available in the sandbox?

uncertainties.question: What happens immediately after Create Account, including any email confirmation or activation?
uncertainties.impact: The sandbox account flow may add one or more steps before Merchant Interface login.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The signup page documents inputs and Create Account but no post-submit state.

uncertainties.question: How is the credential-verification PIN delivered and how long can it take?
uncertainties.impact: It may introduce an external-device or email wait and retry loop.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The setup FAQ says Request and verify a PIN but does not identify channel, expiry, timing, or retry behavior.

uncertainties.question: Why does the current Java Hello World display credential-like example literals without explicitly instructing replacement?
uncertainties.impact: A literal copy may authenticate a shared demo account, fail, or conflict with the page's create-your-account instruction.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The Hello World code and credential FAQ establish the credential parameters, but the prose does not explicitly connect the developer's newly generated values to the two literals in main.

excluded_after_success: Settlement and watching the sandbox transaction settle.
Refund, void, recurring billing, customer profiles, and reporting calls.
Accept.js or Accept Hosted payment-form integration.
Sandbox error and fraud-filter trigger testing.
Merchant account application, PCI scope, production credentials, and go-live.
